narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20—right around the typical level for students in this dataset—your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on past students' data to predict bar exam success on the first try. The model assessed your profile, including your Year 1 school decile ranking of 6th (slightly above average), your lower Year 3 decile of 2nd (well below the typical 5.53 average), a middle family income bracket, an LSAT score of 27.30 (below the dataset average of 36.70), and your plan to attend full-time like most applicants. It ultimately predicted a 40% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting those with stronger signals of passing. The decision hinged on just a few key factors from your background that stood out most to the model. The strongest influence pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 academic decile ranking, as being in the 2nd decile signaled weaker performance relative to peers compared to your stronger Year 1 showing. On a more positive note, your Year 1 decile of 6th worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success and highlighting your early potential. Your undergraduate GPA also helped, nudging the odds toward passing since it matched what the model sees in successful students. Overall, while your early grades and GPA kept things competitive, the drop-off in Year 3 grades was the dominant factor tipping the scales against you, especially when combined with your below-average LSAT. This placed you below the profile of typical admitted students, who tend to maintain consistent or improving deciles and higher test scores. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting your LSAT through targeted prep—perhaps retaking it—and building a narrative around your Year 1 strengths while addressing the Year 3 dip with explanations like challenging courses or personal circumstances. Consider gaining relevant experience, such as internships or legal volunteering, to demonstrate resilience. Retaining a middle-income background like yours isn't a barrier, but pairing it with standout academics could make a big difference next time. With your full-time commitment, you're aligned with the majority who succeed, so persistence will pay off.
